A: Remote teams miss important decisions because discussions are scattered across chats and meetings.
D: Ask someone to take better notes
Use a shared document and hope people update it
Record every meeting and store videos

B.rule: Treat decisions as 'immutable commits' that must be captured at the moment of merge.
B.unlocks: Decision capture, provenance, and retrieval as a first-class workflow step.
B.justification: Software teams avoid ambiguity by merging explicit commits with metadata; decisions can be handled similarly.

C: A one-click 'Decision Commit' flow in chat/meetings that captures the decision, owner, rationale, and links, then auto-updates a decision log and pings affected stakeholders.

A: Customer support tickets bounce between teams because the problem category is unclear at intake.
D: Route everything to a general queue
Require customers to pick a product area
Train agents more on routing rules

B.rule: Treat triage as medical differential diagnosis: ask minimal discriminative questions.
B.unlocks: Adaptive intake that reduces uncertainty with a few high-information prompts.
B.justification: Clinicians narrow causes via questions that maximally separate likely conditions; ticket routing can use the same logic.

C: An adaptive intake wizard that asks 3–5 branching questions chosen by information gain, then routes with confidence + shows 'why' and alternatives for quick override.

A: New hires take months to become productive because context lives in people's heads.

B.rule: Treat onboarding as a guided museum tour with curated exhibits and 'why' plaques.
B.unlocks: Curated paths, progressive disclosure, and narrative reasoning behind systems.
B.justification: Museums compress vast knowledge into memorable routes; teams can curate learning trails with intent.

C: Role-based onboarding 'trails' (2-week routes) that link code, dashboards, and incidents with short 'why it exists' notes and checkpoint tasks that generate reusable artifacts.

A: People forget to follow through on action items after meetings.

B.rule: Treat actions as 'subscriptions' that trigger when prerequisites become true.
B.unlocks: Condition-based nudges instead of time-based nagging.
B.justification: Many tasks stall waiting on dependencies; notifying at the state-change moment is more effective than periodic reminders.

C: Action items become lightweight state machines with dependencies (e.g., approval received, data ready) and notify exactly when the next step becomes unblocked.

A: Teams re-run the same analysis repeatedly because results are hard to find and reuse.

B.rule: Treat analyses as products with packaging: title, ingredients, expiration date, and serving suggestions.
B.unlocks: Standardized metadata, reproducibility, and discoverability by default.
B.justification: Consumer packaging makes items searchable and comparable; analysis outputs need the same affordances.

C: An 'analysis card' standard that auto-captures query/code, dataset versions, key findings, confidence/limitations, and a 1-click rerun button, indexed for search.

A: Engineering incidents repeat because postmortems don’t change day-to-day behavior.

B.rule: Treat safety like aviation: build checklists that are executed at the point of risk.
B.unlocks: Just-in-time guardrails embedded in deploy/ops flows.
B.justification: Aviation safety improved by operational checklists at critical moments, not by reports alone.

C: Convert each postmortem lesson into a small deploy-time or runbook checklist step (with automation where possible) that blocks or warns only when the specific risk signature appears.

A: Product roadmaps thrash because priorities change with every new request.

B.rule: Treat roadmap capacity as a fixed 'budget' and requests as bids in an auction.
B.unlocks: Transparent tradeoffs and opportunity cost visible to requesters.
B.justification: Auctions allocate scarce resources by forcing explicit value signals; roadmap slots are scarce resources.

C: A quarterly 'roadmap auction' where teams spend limited tokens to bid on initiatives, with tokens tied to strategic goals—forcing explicit tradeoffs and reducing ad-hoc thrash.

A: Users abandon signup because verification steps are annoying on mobile.

B.rule: Treat verification as a 'trust ramp' that earns confidence via small proofs.
B.unlocks: Progressive verification that adapts friction to risk.
B.justification: Security can be staged: low-risk accounts require fewer proofs until risk increases.

C: Progressive verification: allow immediate low-risk use after a minimal proof, then request stronger verification only when users attempt sensitive actions (payments, exports, invites).

A: A recommendation system becomes stale because it over-personalizes and stops exploring.

B.rule: Treat the feed as a balanced diet: nutrients (novelty) and comfort foods (relevance).
B.unlocks: Scheduled exploration as a health metric rather than a tuning knob.
B.justification: Diet planning succeeds when variety is intentional and measured, not accidental.

C: A 'novelty nutrition label' that enforces a weekly quota of new categories/creators per user, with explainable tradeoffs and user-adjustable taste boundaries.

A: Model training is slow because data loading and preprocessing bottleneck GPUs.

B.rule: Treat the pipeline like a factory line: remove starvation via buffering and takt time.
B.unlocks: Quantized stages, prefetch buffers, and throughput matching across steps.
B.justification: Factories maximize output by balancing stage times and adding buffers where variability causes starvation.

C: Introduce a 'takt-time profiler' that auto-sizes prefetch/queue buffers per stage and moves variable augmentations into an asynchronous microservice that outputs ready-to-train batches.

A: People avoid giving feedback because it feels awkward and high-stakes.

B.rule: Treat feedback as iterative bug reports on behaviors, not evaluations of people.
B.unlocks: Low-drama, testable, small-scope suggestions with follow-up checks.
B.justification: Bug reports work because they are specific, reproducible, and aimed at improvement, not judgment.

C: A 'behavior bug report' template with reproduction steps (context), observed impact, minimal fix suggestion, and a scheduled quick re-test check-in two weeks later.

A: Users don’t trust AI outputs because they can’t tell when it’s wrong.

B.rule: Treat the system as an instrument that must be calibrated and show error bars.
B.unlocks: Calibration tasks, drift detection, and user-facing uncertainty bounds.
B.justification: Measurement tools build trust by specifying operating ranges and known failure modes.

C: Add a built-in 'calibration mode' that periodically asks the model to solve known check items per domain, then displays a live 'operating range' badge and auto-routes risky queries to safer modes.

A: Teams ship regressions because integration tests don’t cover real-world edge cases.

B.rule: Treat production as a wind tunnel: generate synthetic stress patterns that mimic failure physics.
B.unlocks: Adversarial scenario generation based on observed failure modes.
B.justification: Wind tunnels test against representative stressors derived from physics, not random conditions.

C: A failure-mode-driven scenario generator that learns 'stress signatures' from incidents (timing, payload shapes, concurrency) and auto-builds test cases that replay and mutate those signatures.

A: Warehouse picking is inefficient because workers walk long distances between items.

B.rule: Treat picking like data locality in computing: move frequently co-accessed items closer.
B.unlocks: Co-occurrence clustering and dynamic slotting based on order correlations.
B.justification: Computers speed up by placing frequently accessed data together; warehouses can do the same with item co-picks.

C: Dynamic slotting that clusters items by co-pick graph (not just frequency) and periodically reshuffles a small 'hot zone' to minimize correlated walking distance.

A: A city’s bike lanes are underused because riders feel unsafe at intersections.

B.rule: Treat intersections as negotiation protocols with clear right-of-way signals and timing windows.
B.unlocks: Signal-phase design and physical cues that reduce ambiguity.
B.justification: Protocols reduce collisions by defining explicit states and handshakes; intersections can be redesigned to reduce uncertain negotiation.

C: Introduce protected 'bike-only handshake phases' with early-release signals plus curb-protected corner islands that physically enforce the protocol and reduce conflict points.

A: Online courses have high dropout because learners lose momentum after missing a week.

B.rule: Treat learning like a streak-based rehabilitation plan with relapse-friendly re-entry ramps.
B.unlocks: Gentle re-onboarding pathways that reduce shame and restart friction.
B.justification: Rehab programs succeed by making relapse recovery easy and structured, rather than punishing.

C: A 're-entry lane' that detects missed time and offers a 30-minute recap + one tiny win task, then dynamically reshapes the schedule to restore a realistic cadence.

A: Restaurants waste food because demand is unpredictable day-to-day.
D: Raise prices to reduce demand spikes

B.rule: Treat prep as modular 'base components' that can assemble into multiple dishes late.
B.unlocks: Late-binding menus where final differentiation happens near serving time.
B.justification: Late binding reduces wasted specialized inventory by postponing irreversible choices.

C: Design dishes around shared prepared bases (sauces, grains, veg mixes) and finalize flavor profiles at order time, paired with a real-time 'sell-through' dashboard to steer specials.

A: People miss appointments because they underestimate travel time and context switching.

B.rule: Treat transitions as 'setup time' like in manufacturing, not as free time.
B.unlocks: Automatic setup-time budgeting (packing, walking, mental switching).
B.justification: Production planning accounts for changeover time; human schedules usually don’t, causing chronic underestimation.

C: A calendar layer that learns personal setup-time profiles per location and meeting type, then automatically inserts realistic buffers and proposes swaps when the day becomes infeasible.

A: Open-plan offices are noisy, reducing focus and increasing stress.
D: Let people work from home more

B.rule: Treat sound like network traffic: shape, prioritize, and route it.
B.unlocks: Zoning, throttling, and 'QoS' for conversations vs focus work.
B.justification: Networks handle contention via policies and routing; offices can handle acoustic contention similarly.

C: Acoustic zoning with visible 'conversation lanes' (designated talk pods) and a lightweight reservation + occupancy indicator that nudges traffic away from focus zones in real time.

A: People procrastinate on difficult tasks because the starting cost feels too high.

B.rule: Treat task initiation as static friction: overcome it with a tiny impulse, then sustain motion.
B.unlocks: Ultra-low-bar 'starter actions' that reliably trigger continuation.
B.justification: Physical systems resist starting more than continuing; designing a starter impulse reduces perceived barrier.

C: A '2-minute ignition ritual' that pre-creates the workspace and opens exactly one next file/tab with a single micro-goal, then automatically offers the next action only after momentum is detected.
